artifacts.content:
Hi Mum,

Just grabbing a quiet moment while Oliver is glued to Bluey and Emma’s marking a stack of books at the table.

House hunting has properly ramped up. We’ve basically narrowed it to Devonport and Belmont now. Oliver’s been on the Belmont Primary waitlist since June 2024, and when Emma met the SENCO there on 18 July 2024 she was pretty clear that being in-zone will really help with the extra learning support he needs for his speech delay. So any listing that isn’t Bayswater/Belmont/Devonport-ish and in the right zone just gets binned.

We’re also thinking a lot about you and Dad when we look at places. With your hip and that fall you had at the Tauranga rental back in October 2022 on the horrible steep driveway, we’re only looking at flat sections now – ideally 600 m² or so – so you can walk from the street to the front door without feeling like you’re mountaineering. And with Dad’s minor stroke in March 2024 and all the follow‑up appointments he’s got at MercyAscot in Epsom from July 2024 onwards, we really want a layout with a quiet ground‑floor room and easy bathroom access so he can stay with us when he comes up.

On top of that, there’s all the Oliver stuff to juggle – his anxiety after the bullying at Bayswater Kindy, the Starship and Kari Centre appointments, and trying to keep him close to a good school. No wonder Emma and I end up at the kitchen table most nights with tea, spreadsheets, and realestate.co.nz.

We’ll keep you posted on any houses that look promising. Maybe the next Sunday video call we can screen‑share a couple of listings.

Love,
Michael

Hi Laura,

Long overdue update from the Bayswater chaos HQ.

We’re getting much more specific about what we need in a place. Top of the list is a standalone house with a fully fenced, flat backyard. After the CYF visit in March 2022 when Oliver bolted toward the road, and then the welfare check in August 2023 when the lady at 15 Norwood complained about his screaming, I never want to repeat that. CYF were actually reasonable, but it scared the hell out of us. So: no shared stairwells, no open access, just a secure section where he can meltdown or zoom around without neighbours peering in.

We’re also thinking ahead for Mum. Since her fall on that steep Tauranga driveway and now all the talk about another hip replacement in late 2025, Dr Helen Morris at Tauranga Hospital told us we should only consider flat sites where she can walk easily from street to front door. So we’re focusing on 550–650 m², mostly flat, and definitely not one of those crazy North Shore cliff sections.

Work-wise, I’m still juggling the ASB digital payments stuff plus late‑night calls with London and Singapore. After the formal warning Karen gave me back in August 2023 for missing that 1 a.m. incident bridge, I’m determined that the next house has a proper separate office or sleepout so I can take calls without waking Oliver or being woken by street noise myself.

Otherwise, same old: Saturday open homes, Sunday beach if the weather behaves, and Emma trying to get her planning done around Oliver’s tantrums. We should line up a weekend where you come up and do open homes with us – you’ve got the physio eye for dodgy stairs and weird slopes.

Love,
Mikey

metadata.subject: Fence, flat, and not going mad

artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I wanted to give you a bit more context about why I’m so fixated on a decent home office as we hunt for a house.

Since that panic attack on the Northern Motorway back in August 2022 and starting fortnightly CBT with Dr Emily Rhodes at Auckland City Hospital, I’ve learned that long, unpredictable commutes are a big anxiety trigger. That’s part of why I’m trying to stay within 15–20 minutes of North Wharf and keep working from home 2–3 days a week.

The June 2024 performance plan we agreed, around me having a dedicated workspace because the hot‑desking at Wynyard Quarter was making things worse, has actually helped. On WFH days, though, I’m still in a makeshift corner of the third bedroom at 17 Norwood Road. With Oliver’s meltdowns and the street noise, it’s not ideal for the 1–3 a.m. incident calls with the Singapore vendor or the 2 a.m. London release bridges. After the formal warning in October 2023 for missing that London call, I can’t afford another slip.

There’s also the security side: under the July 2023 equipment agreement, I’ve got around $12k worth of ASB/Visa test POS terminals and sometimes sensitive customer documentation at home for the North Wharf contactless pilot running through late 2024. At the moment they’re in a lockable cupboard wedged next to Oliver’s Lego, which isn’t exactly textbook.

So in the new place I’m aiming for a separate, lockable office or sleepout – quiet enough for overnight calls, secure enough for kit and docs, and far enough from the main living area that Emma and Oliver can actually sleep.

Happy to chat more about how this fits with our flexible working setup.

Cheers,
Michael

House‑hunting master checklist – updated

Suburbs
- Primary focus now: Devonport, Belmont, Bayswater. Belmont especially because Oliver’s been on the Belmont Primary waitlist since June 2024 and the SENCO we spoke to in Feb 2024 about his speech delay and learning support really emphasised being in‑zone.
- Secondary: Takapuna/Milford – still handy to North Wharf and North Shore Hospital (Dad’s heart episode there in Oct 2023 is a reminder to stay within ~20–25 mins).

Property type
- Standalone house only – no apartments/townhouses with shared corridors or stairwells. CYFS/Oranga Tamariki family safety plan from March 2023 after Oliver wandered out of the Glen Innes flats + later welfare check in Aug 2023 at Norwood means we need a fenced section, no shared access.
- Fully fenced yard, childproofable doors/windows because of Oliver’s ASD/bolting and meltdown noise.

Land size / layout
- Aim for 500–650 m², flat or gently sloping. Need room for:
  * Trampoline + swing set (per Dr Ritchie at North Shore Hospital, May 2024 – daily outdoor play for Oliver’s anxiety after Bayswater Kindy bullying).
  * Veggie patch + compost like the Tauranga setup Dad and I built in March 2021 – still chipping away at the $28k IVF loan from Fertility Associates (2020).
  * Future‑proofing for Mum’s hip (Oct 2022 fall + late‑2025 replacement) and Susan’s Parkinson’s (Waikato Hospital Feb 2024) – flat access, possible granny flat.

Other must‑haves
- Separate, quiet, preferably lockable office for late‑night London/Singapore calls and ASB test kit storage.
- In‑zone for Bayswater, Belmont, Devonport or Takapuna Primary; walking distance ideal.
- Within 20–25 mins of Starship/Kari Centre/Auckland City Hospital corridors for Oliver’s appointments and my CBT.

Remember: no leaky‑home risk, no crazy driveways, no giant projects.

Event: Thursday therapy + commute planning

Time: Every Thursday, 7:30–8:15 a.m.
Location: The Tonic Room, Ponsonby
Attendees: Michael

Notes:
- Leave Devonport/Bayswater by ~6:50 a.m. to allow for traffic over the bridge, park near Ponsonby Rd.
- 7:30 a.m. anxiety therapy session with therapist at The Tonic Room – ongoing since the panic attack on the Northern Motorway in August 2022. Still finding the early slot helpful before heading to ASB North Wharf.
- Aim to be at North Wharf office by 9 a.m. latest on these days (walk from Ponsonby or quick bus).

Context for house search:
- Need to keep future home within a sensible radius so Thursdays aren’t a nightmare. Ideally Devonport or Takapuna so I can bike/ferry on non‑therapy days but still drive to Ponsonby in time for 7:30.
- Combine with fortnightly CBT sessions with Dr Emily Rhodes at Auckland City Hospital (separate calendar series) – try to cluster city commitments on the same days to reduce motorway time and keep anxiety manageable.
- Remember late‑night London payments team calls the night before; if there’s a 1–3 a.m. incident bridge, block out the 10–11 a.m. slot for recovery so I’m not a zombie in stand‑ups.

To bring:
- Notebook with points from the week (house‑hunt stress, Oliver’s meltdowns, work pressure from Visa pilot at North Wharf lab).

Hi Susan,

Just wanted to loop you in on where Emma and I have got to with the house search, since so much of it now is about making things work for both sides of the family.

We’re focusing on standalone character homes – nothing in big apartment complexes – ideally with a separate sleepout or at least space to add one. Part of that is thinking about Robert. After his drink‑driving conviction at Hamilton District Court in August 2024 and the alcohol interlock/community detention stuff, his lawyer was pretty clear he shouldn’t be living alone in the Hamilton unit for a while. If we can find a place with a sleepout, he could stay with us for a few months while things settle.

We’re also factoring in your own health. After the February 2024 appointment with the neurologist at Waikato Hospital and the early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis, we really want a flat 550–650 m² section so we can eventually put a small granny flat out the back with shorter, easier access – no big flights of stairs or long slogs from street to front door. That lines up with what we’re already doing for Mum and her hips.

On top of that, Oliver’s needs are driving a lot of decisions: fully fenced, very private backyard (after the CYFS welfare check in August 2023 courtesy of the neighbour at 15 Norwood), and in‑zone for somewhere like Belmont or Bayswater so we don’t have to start from scratch with his speech support.

It sounds like a ridiculous wishlist when I write it all down, but it actually helps us say no quickly to the wrong places. We’ll send through any listings that look promising – would value your engineer’s eye on the photos.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: How your health (and Robert’s) is shaping our house hunt

artifacts.content:
Future‑planning brain dump – Mum, Dad, Susan, Oliver

Mum (Margaret)
- Breast cancer: chemo at Tauranga Hospital, due to finish around Nov 2025. Need a ground‑floor quiet room where she can stay for a night or two after each round (from May 2025 onwards if they start then). Ideally a separate, calm space away from Oliver’s noise.
- Hips: fall at steep Tauranga rental Oct 2022 + planned hip replacement late 2025. Flat driveway and short, gentle path from street to front door are non‑negotiable.

Dad (David)
- Heart episode at North Shore Hospital Oct 2023 + minor stroke March 2024. Follow‑ups at MercyAscot Epsom from July 2024. Need a temporary bedroom on the ground floor with easy bathroom access for his visits from Tauranga.
- Try to stay within 20–25 mins of North Shore Hospital just in case.

Susan
- Early‑stage Parkinson’s (Waikato Hospital, Feb 2024). Plan for potential granny flat on a 550–650 m² flat section so she can move in later, minimal stairs.

Oliver
- ASD diagnosis (Dr Karen Liu, Starship, May 2023) + on the autism assessment clinic waitlist again since March 2024 for updated review.
- CYF involvement: March 2022 road‑bolting incident, Aug 2023 welfare check after neighbour at 15 Norwood complained about screaming.
- Needs: fully fenced, private backyard; quiet cul‑de‑sac to reduce noise overwhelm; close to Starship/Kari Centre (10–20 mins) for psych and OT; flat lawn for trampoline/sensory garden (inspired by Sarah Ng’s Starship sensory garden Aug 2023).

Me/Emma
- My anxiety (Northern Motorway panic Aug 2022, CBT with Dr Rhodes) + couples counselling at Shore Therapy Feb 2024 and “more space helps” advice. Standalone house with proper office/spare room remains key.

metadata.title: Future-planning – family needs for new house

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Patterson <michael.patterson@example.com>
To: Laura Jane Patterson <laura.patterson@example.com>
Date: 2024-09-18
Subject: House hunting jigsaw (Mum, schools, and garages)

Hey Lau,

Quick download on the house hunt because it’s turning into a full-on logistics puzzle.

On one front, we’re telling agents we’d really prefer to stay in Bayswater or move to Takapuna or Milford so Mum can practically base herself with us after her knee replacement surgery at Tauranga Hospital in March 2025. The idea is she stays here while she rehabs and I keep doing the weekday school runs to Bayswater School, even on the evenings when Emma’s tied up with late parent–teacher conferences over at Stanley Bay School. Being close to both schools will matter once Oliver’s properly settled.

At the same time, we’re only looking at a free‑standing family home with a fully self‑contained sleep‑out or minor dwelling out the back so Mum can move up from Tauranga once her breast cancer chemotherapy finishes in November 2025. With her being immunocompromised for a while, anything like an attached townhouse or apartment complex with shared corridors is off the table.

On the very unglamorous practical side, after my minor drink‑driving conviction from that incident on Lake Road, Auckland in June 2022, I’m still on a zero‑alcohol licence and rely on Emma to drive me to early‑morning meetings when I’m wrecked from late work calls. So we’re adding “flat driveway + internal‑access garage” to the must‑have list so it’s easy and safe for her to load Oliver and my half‑asleep self into the car.

Will send you a couple of listings later – would love your physio eye on access/steps for Mum.

M

metadata.subject: House hunting jigsaw (Mum, schools, and garages)

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Patterson <michael.patterson@example.com>
To: Margaret Patterson <margaret.patterson@example.com>
Date: 2024-10-02
Subject: Looking for a place with you in mind

Hi Mum,

Just wanted to reassure you that we really are factoring your health into this house hunt.

We’re asking the agent to focus on places within about a 10‑minute walk of either Devonport Primary School or Takapuna Primary School. With Emma planning to go back to 4 days a week teaching from Term 1 2025, we need school drop‑offs to be as simple as possible. That way I can still manage monthly trips – driving down to Tauranga once a month – without everything falling over here.

We’re also being upfront that we want a proper standalone home with a self‑contained unit or sleep‑out so there’s space for you to move up from Tauranga when the timing’s right. I keep thinking back to February 2024 at Tauranga Hospital, when you got the early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis on top of everything else. Then there’s the breast cancer chemotherapy you’re working through now, and how you’ll still be a bit immunocompromised even after it finishes in November 2025. Having your own quiet space, without shared corridors or lots of people coming and going, feels really important.

Don’t worry about being a “burden” – you’re not. We’re just trying to design a setup where Emma’s work, Oliver’s school, and my visits to you all fit together.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Looking for a place with you in mind

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Patterson <michael.patterson@example.com>
To: David Patterson <david.patterson@example.com>
Date: 2024-09-25
Subject: Why we’re leaning Milford/Browns Bay

Hi Dad,

Quick note to explain why most of the listings I’ve been sending you are in Milford and Browns Bay.

We’re leaning toward Milford or Browns Bay because they’re still firmly on the North Shore but a bit more affordable than Devonport, and we really want to stay within about 20–25 minutes of North Shore Hospital. Ever since your heart episode and the emergency visit to North Shore Hospital back in October 2023, it’s been at the back of my mind that if anything like that happens again while you’re up here, we need to be able to get you there quickly without battling traffic across the bridge.

The good thing is that those suburbs still give us reasonable ferry/bus options into the CBD for me, and they’re not too far from Bayswater or Takapuna if we end up doing a lot of back‑and‑forth for Oliver’s schooling and when Mum comes up post‑chemo.

I’ve also put a recurring reminder in my calendar to drive down to Tauranga once a month so I can see Mum while she’s working through everything from the Parkinson’s diagnosis to the chemo. Being on the Shore but still close to the hospital feels like the right balance between you two and our own little crew here.

I’ll send you a couple of Trade Me links later – interested in what you think of the sections and access.

Love,
Michael

From: Michael Patterson <m.patterson@example.com>
To: Emma Patterson <emma.patterson@example.com>
Date: 2025-02-16 21:18
Subject: Refining our must-haves before Saturday’s open homes

Hey Em,

Just been going through the latest listings in Devonport, Belmont and Takapuna and I think we need to be a bit more explicit about what we’re actually targeting so we don’t waste another Saturday like last week.

Land size: I keep coming back to that sweet spot of a flat 450–550 m² section. Anything smaller feels like we’re compromising on outdoor space for Oliver, and anything much bigger starts to look like a maintenance project. If we can find something in that range, we can properly fence off a play area for him and still have room to build a small sensory garden, similar to the one his paediatric OT, Sarah Ng, set up at Starship Children’s Hospital, Auckland during his developmental delay assessment and those sensory therapy sessions back in August 2023. He was so much calmer there; it’d be amazing to recreate that at home.

Location: I’ve added a new filter in my head – we should aim for places that are realistically within a 10–15 minute drive of the Kari Centre in Greenlane. With Oliver’s weekly child psychology sessions now locked in there from November 2024, and those 4:30 p.m. appointments after school, we really can’t be stuck in traffic for half an hour each way.

Let’s sit down tomorrow night after Oliver’s in bed and shortlist 3–4 places max for this weekend.

M.

metadata.subject: Refining our must-haves before Saturday’s open homes

artifacts.content:
Title: House-hunting non‑negotiables – updated
Date: 2025-02-20
Author: Michael Andrew Patterson

1. Section + outdoor space
- Aim for ~500 m² flat section (acceptable range 450–550 m²).
- Must be fully fenceable so Oliver can scooter and ride his bike safely.
- Space for small veggie patch + a sensory garden.
  → Idea: replicate the layout from Starship Children’s Hospital, Auckland – the one paediatric OT Sarah Ng used during Oliver James Patterson’s developmental delay assessment and follow‑up sensory therapy sessions in August 2023. Include similar textures (pavers, bark, herbs, lavender) and a quiet corner where he can decompress.

2. Commute + services
- CBD commute: target 20–30 mins off‑peak, max ~50 mins in peak.
- Critical: proximity to Kari Centre, Greenlane.
  → Need new house to be within a 10–15 minute drive so we can reliably make Oliver’s weekly child psychology sessions that started November 2024.
  → Remember: his after-school 4:30 p.m. appointments are a pressure point – he gets wound up if stuck in traffic too long.

3. Layout + rooms
- 3+ bedrooms, with one that can be a dedicated home office.
- Warm, dry, good natural light; no obvious moisture issues or leaky-home risk.
- Indoor–outdoor flow from living area to deck/patio so we can supervise Oliver easily.

4. Access + visitors
- Avoid steep driveways/sections so grandparents (Tauranga/Hamilton) can visit comfortably.

Event: Oliver – psych sessions @ Kari Centre
Organizer: Michael Andrew Patterson
Date: Recurring weekly from 2024-11-05 (Tuesdays)
Time: 4:30 p.m. appointments (arrive by 4:20 p.m.)
Location: Kari Centre, Greenlane, Auckland
Attendees: Michael, Oliver James Patterson – son

Description / Notes:
- Ongoing weekly child psychology sessions that started in November 2024.
- Once we move from Bayswater, Auckland, new house must be within a realistic 10–15 minute drive of Kari Centre so after-school appointments don’t become a mission.
- Plan: pick Oliver up straight after school, quick snack in the car, then drive to Greenlane. Aim to leave home no later than 3:45 p.m. to allow for traffic.
- If commute from new place regularly pushes past 20 mins, reconsider suburb choice.

House-hunt implications:
- Prioritise Devonport / Belmont / Takapuna / Milford streets with good motorway or busway access towards Greenlane.
- Remember how frazzled he got on the longer drives from Bayswater during the trial block of sessions – shorter, predictable trips keep him calmer.
- Add note to open-home checklist: estimate drive time "New house → Kari Centre (Oliver psych appt)" in peak after-school traffic.

Reminders:
- Set alert at 3:15 p.m. on session days.
- Bring OT/sensory toolkit inspired by Sarah Ng’s Starship Children’s Hospital setup (fidget toys, noise-cancelling headphones, chewable necklace).

Notes to self:

- Email Address: michael.patterson.nz@gmail.com
- Signed: Michael A. Patterson
- Date Day: 2.0
- Date Month: 3.0
- Date Year: 2026.0
